data.title: What CEOs Had To Say At Davos
data.description:
There is no shortage of takes on the political speeches at Davos for the World Economic Forum, 2026. I'll be skipping those here; I've already covered Greenland and the tariff issue; you can read that here.

Instead, I wanted to cover what some of the business leaders who spoke at Davos had to say and what we can take away from what they had to say; these are, namely, Microsoft's (MSFT) Satya Nadella and JPMorgan's (JPM) Jamie Dimon.

Nadella Argues There is No AI Bubble

You can watch the conversation here.

The central theme of the conversation with Larry Fink of BlackRock (BLK) and Satya Nadella was focused around AI and specifically the diffusion of AI, not only agents operating in the economy but also infrastructure like data centers (which he calls "token factories" in his metaphor) and electrical grid expansion.

Nadella tries to lay out the case that there is no AI bubble, largely because he believes that there is existing--and that there will be future--demand for the supply of AI capacity being created. He explains that one of the ways that they will ensure demand for AI is that they are diffusing the infrastructure and that Microsoft is spending 50% of their hyperscaler budget on ex-U.S. AI infrastructure to this end. Specifically here, he evokes the Global South and how building data centers in ex-U.S. geographies is important for the overall diffusion of AI services and how Microsoft will make money moving forward.

And it's true, of the 8,000+ data centers currently operating around the world, over 7,000 are in suboptimal climates, built not because of efficiency, but because of national sovereignty. It makes sense to build a data center in a place that's too hot for optimal data center operations if your whole country is too hot, and you need data centers in your country. Mandela discusses how critical sovereignty is in his forum and how countries around the world are going to continue to want and fight for data sovereignty from the model providers and hyperscalers.

Here's a map of those 8,000 data centers and global temperatures for reference. Most of them are still in the U.S. and Europe.

Nadella argues that this is ultimately a good thing because the diffusion of AI will enable more people across the world to use it and to create economic benefits with it, a central outcome necessary for the AI build-out to not be a bubble. He calls the charges about GDP growth being led by AI capex as being a "point in time" argument and says that it is still too early to see AI productivity in the economic data.

One of the central points that Nadella makes and attempts to lay out for the audience is that token costs, which largely drive the cost of operating AI models, are falling. As we expand the energy grid and expand our supply of AI capacity, token prices will continue to fall. This oversupply of tokens and the build-out of energy grid capacity to generate said tokens have already been driving the price down. The price of a million tokens halved in the last year.

Nadella's argument finishes with the point that I believe most investors would consider actionable: he is bullish on emerging markets and small firms. Nadella says that this diffusion of AI and the driving down of costs to use and to deploy AI systems means that they will become such a commodity that the most AI-enabled and highest adoption firms will dominate future markets.

He extends this to emerging economies because they often have lower regulatory barriers and can adapt to the incoming technology faster; he says that this time, unlike in other tech buildouts, emerging markets are getting access to AI tools at the same time as developed markets.

At the same time, small firms stand to gain against large firms since they are able to be more flexible and integrate AI tools with less friction. Nadella said that new workflows will benefit small players first, as they're more easily able to integrate them, and that there may be a new class of firms coming out of this AI build-out.

I'm not sure that I buy that last one; I still don't think that we're in for a great rotation. But the idea is that if the AI build-out is not truly a bubble, then the beneficiaries will be spread out across markets, and that is a charge for portfolio diversity. Nadella is not predicting that Microsoft and the other hyperscalers will eat the world -- he's predicting the opposite. Emerging market and small-cap exposure may be preferable to large caps if he's right, as they have a lot of catching up to do valuation-wise.

Dimon Was Much More Hesitant

You can watch the conversation here.

The first thing Jamie Dimon discussed was how they're implementing AI at JPM and how he believes that it's transformational in the workforce because of its ability to be applied to basically every facet of his business as a non-tech business. This leans on a point Nadella brought up earlier in the day that one of the hallmarks of the AI build-out not being a bubble is the rate at which AI is being applied to non-tech businesses. Dimon confirming it at JPM is a good sign for the AI bulls.

What wasn't a good sign for the AI bulls was how much hedging Dimon did on what the future will look like; he mentioned how many jobs are likely to be lost and that the government will likely need to step in and help people with retraining or early retirements, something that historically has not gone very well in past iterations. His great fear here was that AI progress is moving too fast and that a displaced workforce will not be able to adapt fast enough and that the economy will not be nimble enough to absorb them elsewhere.

When asked about how AI is impacting the workforce at J.P. Morgan itself, he mentioned that it's unlikely for there to be mass layoffs, but that he does expect the JPM workforce to shrink. Dimon didn't explicitly mention the "boiling frog" metaphor, but he did make the note of, "We would do [layoffs] slowly."

For reference, JPM is at its all-time high total employee count, and Dimon may have signaled that it just peaked.

The second half of the discussion went over economic policies.

Specifically, for the financial sector, Dimon said that President Trump's 10% credit card cap command would be disastrous for the economy (and therefore his firm, which is a huge credit card issuer via Chase). He said it would remove credit access from up to 80% of Americans, who would no longer be eligible if the highest interest rate was capped at 10%. I believe these figures because they likely come from JPM's internals having calculated the effects of this. He was very confident in his assertions that this would be terrible for JPM and financials at large.

He said that not only would it hurt his firm as the issuer, but also the real economy: retailers, restaurants, municipalities, etc., because much of consumer spending is currently being done on credit. That spending loss would be economically disastrous.

The most actionable piece of Dimon's talk at Davos was this: Financials are currently experiencing overreach from the administration and could have a large negative catalyst in front of them if Congress does codify or Trump pushes an executive order to limit credit card rates.

This makes me hesitant to overweight financial companies or sectors, although it may depend on the particular company you're considering. For example, my colleague Julia Ostian made a very good argument for why this news shouldn't affect one's view of American Express (AXP).

Conclusion

There is a lot of bluster at Davos, and many of the conversations and speeches I listened to were largely political in nature or circled back to the same topic that I think Nadella covered best: how AI will shape the economy.

Dimon introduced some hesitancy and a little more realism into the conversation, which I think is necessary. But largely, the tone was bullish on AI overall and the current trajectory of mega caps in the economy, which drive the S&P 500.

There's a lot of optimism from the voices I have heard at Davos about AI and its impacts on the economy, and I think it overshadows the hesitancy and doom that I heard. That may just be financial elitist techno-optimism on display, but it also seems grounded in some reality -- one that could play out in front of us. It seems current optimism largely favors emerging markets and small caps, while disfavoring financial companies and those sectors with deep regulatory environments.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: SPMO: What Happens When Momentum Wanes
data.description:
The Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) is one of my favorite funds. Its momentum strategy consistently outperforms the S&P 500 (SPY), and it does so without over-reliance on the Mag 7 stocks or the technology sector.

However, momentum has recently waned in the broader market. The SPY is trading at the same prices it traded at back in October, and SPMO has made no progress since early August. Furthermore, rotations have generally been out of strong-performing stocks and into value. This article looks at what could happen to SPMO if this situation continues.

SPMO Basics

SPMO was launched in 2015. While it has outperformed SPY since inception, it only really distanced itself in 2024 and 2025.

The recent growth hasn't gone unnoticed, and the fund's AUM has ballooned in recent years.

SPMO tracks the S&P 500 Momentum Index, which is designed to capture stocks exhibiting strong price momentum. This momentum can be sustained in certain environments and lead to huge moves. SPMO will catch the likes of Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR) when they are making significant rallies.

SPMO Methodology

SPMO is reconstituted twice a year, in March and September. It holds around 100 stocks, and these can be changed quite considerably. The last reconstitution in September added 41 stocks and deleted 40 stocks, while also changing the weights of retained stocks. This led to a very different top 10 and sector exposure, with technology increasing to a 34% weighting.

I sometimes have my doubts about the additions and deletions. The methodology primarily rewards a large 12-month price change and therefore adds stocks that can look stretched. But either you believe in momentum—and its tendency to persist—or you don't.

Here is a deeper look at the exact methodology:

First, the momentum value is calculated. This is based on the 12-month price change (excluding the most recent month).

The momentum value is then adjusted for volatility. Lower volatility in uptrends is rewarded, as is higher volatility in downtrends.

A winsorized Z-score is calculated to standardize the values. The stocks with the top 100 scores are selected.

Weights are calculated by market cap combined with momentum score, with a 9% cap.

This is the current top 10 and sector exposure:

Meta (META) has performed poorly since the last rebalancing. And Oracle (ORCL) was a poorly timed addition. But that is the risk of the strategy, and for every ORCL that reverses lower, there could be an NVDA that keeps trending higher.

Consequences of Low Momentum

Short-term momentum was extremely high in mid-2025 but has cooled off since late July. SPMO traded below the August peak last week and has generally moved sideways for around 4 months.

It has underperformed SPY since the end of October.

Of course, this could be a much-needed rest after a huge rally. The issue is what could happen to SPMO's portfolio if broader market momentum stays low.

If stocks were to generally tread water until March, SPMO would still have some valuable data to work with, as it uses the price changes over the previous 12 months (excluding the most recent month). There have been some significant moves from the April '25 lows that will be considered in its calculations.

Any stocks that have returned near to the April lows will likely be cut. That could mean META, ORCL, and Netflix (NFLX) will be replaced. Alphabet (GOOGL) could be a big addition.

It would get really interesting if 2026 is flattish into September, as that would mean the calculations will be based on low 12-month price changes. Midterm years do tend to dip over Q2 and Q3, and technology stocks in the Nasdaq (QQQ) can be generally weaker.

That could lead to higher momentum scores in recently rotationally favored stocks and sectors and lead to major changes in SPMO's portfolio. But what if that's just when tech and the usual leaders take off again? SPMO would not adjust for another 6 months.

This is all speculation, of course, but I like to think ahead. SPMO does have flaws, and the way it can completely change nearly half of its holdings based on the previous 12-month price changes means it could adjust just when the market is changing again.

It's not that SPMO will suddenly become a "Sell." Indeed, SPMO has been very good at adapting to various market conditions, and if tech underperforms for any length of time, the reconstitutions will be beneficial. Furthermore, it tends to hold up well during drawdowns. However, it can underperform, and not every year will be like 2024 or 2025.

Conclusions

Momentum in the S&P 500 is stalling. It may be temporary, but if it continues, it could lead to some major changes in SPMO's holdings and a flatter performance in 2026. My concern is that it could make major portfolio changes at the wrong times based on the previous 12-month's data. What if it added ORCL and increased Broadcom's (AVGO) weighting right at the highs? And what if it adjusts to a value/defensive tilt just when the market shifts back towards growth/tech again?

That's only speculation at this point and isn't likely to happen until the September rebalancing. For now, SPMO remains a key part of my ETF holdings.

data.title: RIET: High Distributions, High Erosion, High Expectations
data.description:
When it comes to REITs, I really appreciate competitive distributions, and I must admit that at the beginning it was precisely this factor that made me research the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield ETF (RIET). Only later did I realize that RIET is not a proxy of the classic market-cap weighted REIT index; it shifts toward less traded segments, where lower pricing efficiency makes them … (in my opinion) much more sensitive to credit dynamics.

And if on the one hand this seems to bring genuinely higher distributions, on the other hand, as often happens, the price suffers. And yet, a change that has occurred in the credit market could work in favor of RIET.

But before discussing this aspect …

What Is RIET (Really)?

RIET passively tracks the Hoya Capital High Dividend Yield Index, focused on high income from U.S. real estate while maintaining the objective of cash flow stability, without a perfect alignment with the income produced. Following somewhat the philosophy shared by the CIO, it is an income machine, and the dividend should be seen as the central element of the investor’s experience.

Here, participating in the fund entails bearing an Expense Ratio of 0.50%, to which is added a median 30-day bid-ask spread of 0.21%.

Distribution

Moving straight to the yield, we know that the expense ratio is well covered by an Index Dividend Yield of ~10.1%, corresponding to a 30-day SEC Yield of ~9.6%, which is distributed monthly.

A dividend that I must admit has been pleasantly stable, a characteristic that deserves recognition and that is not always so common. After all, RIET is not constrained to distribute only NII. Therefore, management is able to handle cash flow stability well.

The Strategy

The index achieves these results starting from the macro-universe of U.S. REITs and real estate operating companies, including both common and preferred shares, and following a segmentation process that leads to a final result a selection of:

  • 10 Dividend Champions, selected based on some key criteria: high yield, relatively contained financial leverage, and adequate market capitalization.
  • 10 Large Cap REITs.
  • 25 Mid Cap REITs.
  • 25 Small Cap REITs.
  • 30 preferred securities.

The components are included in the index according to an equal-weight methodology within each category, with semiannual rebalancing.

The result is a portfolio that shows a turnover of 36% and that tends to constantly rotate toward:

  • higher yields.
  • securities often in situations of stress, with the risk of “yield trap.”

This approach clearly diverges from the concept of high dividend aristocrats and places the index more in the area of high dividend opportunistic.

Holding Distribution

At this point, it makes sense to move on to scrutinize the distribution: let us start from the assumption that it consists of 100 securities; a rather peculiar structure, which manages to combine both common and preferreds. From the latest Schedule of Investments (SOI) as of May 31, 2025 it can be observed that about 88% is Common stocks and the remaining (almost 11%) Preferred stocks.

At the same time, 50 securities out of 100 belong to the mid and small-cap category. As I wish to emphasize, their selection took place without resorting to a quality screening on fundamentals, avoiding the analysis of parameters such as ROIC and FCF.

Which makes RIET, in my honest view, much more “mortgage/credit” than the average reader thinks.

Expectations?

At the risk level, I expected a more pronounced volatility in relative terms, precisely because of the strong presence of small/mid-cap solutions. Here an interesting comparison arises with VNQ, which instead contradicts my expectations. RIET’s sector diversification reduces annualized volatility (a metric I prefer over standard deviation).

Moving to the momentum side, it is as I expected, the 3-year performance is weighed down by capital losses for RIET.

Looking at its history, the NAV tends to decline over time in the presence of high distributions, and discount phases coincide with NAV drawdowns, while distributions remain relatively stable. And this is the main difference compared to VNQ, which has lower yield, fewer mREITs or preferreds.

My Opinion

This divergence in performance was mainly caused by the abrupt shift in monetary policy in 2022. With REIT exposures that are more sensitive to this cycle, and aiming (following the CIO’s words) to maintain a constant distribution, it is natural that the ETF suffers in terms of capital.

But let us try to simply ask ourselves:

What are we witnessing in the credit market that could be amplified over the course of 2026? In my view, these two elements:

  • Mortgage rates are declining.

  • Treasury yields / bond yields have instead remained stable.

This implies that the spread between mortgage rates and yields is widening. Evidence that is usually negative, but that in this context could tell another story.

Far from being a stalemate situation, the current environment favors REITs, driven primarily by the decline in financing costs. Therefore, I believe it is likely that the market prices RIET anticipating stable or growing FFO/AFFO flows, thus ensuring better protection for dividends.

On the yield side, instead, a more complex discussion opens up: if yields were expected to rise, this would be negative both in terms of opportunity cost and expectations for mortgage rates. But stable yields, in my view, do not necessarily indicate a mechanical downward pressure on RIET. On the contrary, they leave room for the market to price in potential rate cuts by the Fed. For this reason, over the past month, RIET has been gaining momentum.

Risk

A situation that can be reversed and also read as a risk. I have discussed this in depth here as well, with a good discussion in the comments about it. After all, why are mortgage rates falling while yields are not? Because the Trump administration is injecting liquidity into the system through what is effectively an unconventional QE that has precisely the objective of easing the burden of these rates. Therefore, political expression in some way “forces” this decline, while the neutral expression of the market, which is instead represented by yields, reflects concerns about expected inflation and keeps yields stable (and high). The risk is that the conditions for a Fed cut are not in place, and that the adjustment comes positively from mortgage rates, which would invalidate my thesis.

Conclusion

To conclude, I believe the appropriate rating for RIET is a BUY. This is because the drivers that guide its performance seem to be turning in a positive direction for RIET. Judging by expectations, this regime shift could allow RIET to regain that stability in capital terms and those competitive distributions that an income investor seeks in this segment of the market.

data.title: HEQT: One Of The Few Hedged Equity ETFs That Has Done Well
data.description:
In a 2025 study with the title "Rebuffed: An Empirical Review of Buffer Funds," the authors present findings that buffer funds that use derivatives or other tactical strategies to achieve equity-like returns often fail to achieve their desired outcomes and are generally unsuitable for the typical investor. The article cites things like using cash and treasuries to achieve the same or often better results than buffer funds, as inefficiencies in premium payouts and high management fees can eat away at returns. But that doesn't mean all buffer funds will suffer from this fate.

The Simplify Hedged Equity ETF (HEQT) is one fund that has offered better risk-adjusted returns since its inception compared to a 60/40 allocation to an S&P 500 index fund and short-term U.S. treasury bills. Its zero-cost collar strategy has noticeably allowed HEQT to offer strong protection against downsides at the cost of upside for more steady portfolio growth. Investors, however, need to consider whether this lower volatility is truly what they need. In many cases, downside protection is most important to manage specific cases where maximum loss numbers are known. HEQT does not provide enough specifics to act as a reliable hedge, but its performance has shown the fund to potentially be a valuable complement holding in a traditional 60/40 equity/bond portfolio.

HEQT's Steady Growth and Low Volatility

A common argument against buying buffer/low volatility ETFs is that a combination of whatever asset you want to own plus short-term U.S. treasuries will achieve the same result, especially after management fees. Under this benchmark, HEQT has done very well since its inception. In a backtest done on Portfolio Visualizer with an initial $10K investment and dividends reinvested since December 2021, HEQT has delivered essentially the same returns as a 60/40 portfolio in the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) while offering lower volatility and better downside protection. The 60/40 portfolio, for example, experienced maximum drawdown levels at minus 14.07%, while HEQT held steady at minus 10.56%. Similarly, HEQT's standard deviation levels were lower by 129 basis points.

What's particularly attractive with HEQT, however, is that it offers a unique strategy to hedge against downturns that give investors the capability to diversify away from a traditional 60/40 equity/bond allocation. In this bond allocation, intermediate and longer-duration bonds were typically used instead of short-term treasuries due to their ability to change value and often offset changes in the equity market. In 2008 and 2020, for example, intermediate bonds in the form of the iShares 7-10 Year Treasury Bond ETF (IEF) had a negative correlation with the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). When SPY was crashing, IEF was rising in value and could meaningfully offset overall portfolio losses compared to short-term U.S. treasuries, whose price hardly changes.

But as we saw in 2022, a negative correlation between bonds and equities is not guaranteed. Both the bond and equity markets suffered significant losses during 2022. A 60/40 portfolio in VOO and IEF had similar downsides as SPY without anywhere near the same upside capture. HEQT, on the other hand, significantly outperformed a 60/40 VOO and IEF portfolio.

The implications of this are that HEQT can be a valuable complement to your traditional 60/40 equity and bond portfolio. Equities and bonds don't always have a negative correlation, so HEQT's derivative strategy can be a strong complement to further diversify and reduce the volatility in your portfolio.

HEQT's Strategy for Lower Volatility

To further understand how HEQT is a good complement to a 60/40 equity and bond allocation, we must look at how HEQT operates. This is an ETF that holds an S&P 500 index fund, the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), and employs a zero-cost collar options strategy. A zero-cost collar options strategy is where one buys a protective put and then sells call options and put options further out-of-the-money ("OTM") to cover the cost of the protective puts. It is a strategy that will cap your upsides but limit your losses for a theoretically smoother risk-return profile than the underlying itself. In practice, HEQT is one of the few funds that have effectively been able to accomplish this goal so far.

There are a few defining characteristics of HEQT's trading strategy that help us evaluate the suitability of HEQT as an investment. Unlike some funds, HEQT does provide a defining range of the protection it will provide. In its prospectus, the fund states that its put option spread is generally maintained to protect the fund from a 5-20% decrease in the S&P 500. HEQT will also ladder its positions three months out every quarter. We can take a look at HEQT's holdings at the time of writing this article to visualize the various scenarios with the fund. There are currently three collar positions in HEQT. One expiring February 20, one expiring March 20, and one expiring April 17.

In each collar position, there are three option positions. The February 20 collar position, for example, has a short call at a strike price of 6925, a short put at 5275, and a long put at 6240. The simplified understanding is that if the underlying S&P 500 rises above 6925, this collar position will not be able to capture the continued upside. In return, however, it will protect you from downsides between 6240 and 5275. If the underlying asset falls below 5275, the collar position will be exposed again due to its short put position, but the long put will have already offset a significant portion of the losses.

Key Risks

With HEQT's strategy in mind, the first key risk to discuss is how HEQT will cap your upside. If you refer back to the various backtests studying HEQT, you will notice that while HEQT has done a good job of protecting your downside, it has come at a cost to your upside. And what these past few years have shown is that the rebound can often exceed the downside significantly. Since HEQT's inception, SPY's annualized returns were 12.00% compared to HEQT's 9.17% despite its significantly larger drawdowns and volatility.

That said, it is the S&P 500's phenomenal performance in the last few years that may lead one to seriously consider HEQT. After what has felt like a three-year-long bull run (minus the correction in April 2025), doubts have emerged surrounding the capabilities of AI-related companies to continue delivering record-smashing growth. Profits from big tech companies have fallen due to much higher capex for AI investment, and yet, valuations were still continuing to grow at the end of 2025, leading to questions on whether we are in an AI bubble.

Those concerned about being in a bubble might find HEQT attractive to protect their assets from the bubble bursting to some extent. But that leads us to a discussion on the second risk of HEQT.

An important consideration when seeking hedged investments is to look at whether you need them or not in the first place. Hedges are best suited for specific scenarios where factors like maximum loss values are known. The ultimate goal with a hedge is to guarantee that certain worst-case scenarios do not happen. While HEQT specifies that it aims to protect the fund from a 5-20% drop in the S&P 500, that range is still very big. If you need downside protection within that range or higher, there is no guarantee that HEQT can offer it. And for long investors who do not have specific capital requirements in specific timeframes, they're often better off investing in the underlying asset itself. Rebounds and growth in the underlying S&P 500 typically outpace any downturn in the market as long as the U.S. economy grows over the long run.

Additionally, the 5-20% protection is not a guarantee either. It's a target that the fund managers aim for, and as an actively managed fund, it's a target that the fund managers may not always be able to hit.

A Complement to Traditional Allocations

HEQT's track record has been limited to a little more than the last four years, but in that time, it has displayed good performances that can help investors justify holding it as a complement to a traditional portfolio of equities and bonds. As shown in 2022, the use of derivatives from HEQT had created a significantly better result than holding bonds and equities when the two developed a positive correlation. Shifting an equal portion of both equities and bonds into HEQT can result in much better performance.

I rate HEQT a Buy, but do consider your position and whether it is truly valuable. Growth investors are generally better off riding the volatility and experiencing full upsides, while investors looking for specific hedges may not find HEQT suitable for their purposes. It is mostly for those with traditional equity bond allocations where HEQT can make those portfolios perform more efficiently and add a layer of diversification.

data.title: BTGD: The Financial Repression Regime
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

Currency devaluation is a phenomenon that affects all investors and forces us to invest our savings in products that outperform inflation. To this end, we will analyze the STKd 100% Bitcoin & 100% Gold ETF (BTGD).

From my point of view, there are two different types of inflation. The first, which we are all familiar with, is inflation in goods and services, which causes a loss of purchasing power when we buy because the prices of these goods or services rise. The second, less well-known type is asset inflation or monetary inflation. This measures the growth in money supply, which in turn can produce inflation that is not included in the current inflation of goods and services. This is known as asset inflation, which causes financial assets to rise, not because of their improved performance or increase in value, but because they have to be brought into line with the new prices attached to a fiat currency, which continues to fall. The image below clearly illustrates my previous comments.

To try to alleviate this inflationary problem, the fund manager Quantify Funds proposes a different approach. Seek exposure to gold and Bitcoin at the same time, using leverage, to form a portfolio that serves as a hedge against the inflation (price and monetary) described above. Readers should be reminded that simply investing their money is not enough. These investments must yield returns above the inflation rates mentioned above. And since the highest of these averages 8% per year, you need to expose yourself to assets that really provide you with the highest returns on the market. That is why the fund manager focuses on gold (a traditional store of value) and bitcoin (a scarce asset), which have played this role very effectively in recent times.

Regarding the thesis, I recommend a strong purchase of the ETF. Based on my current view of things, the world's high debt levels and the path that both governments and central banks around the world are taking, I believe that the value of fiat currencies will continue to decline in the future and that it will be more necessary than ever to invest in order to maintain people's wealth. Consider also that debt and inflation already act as a political tool, since it is the debt-issuing governments themselves that benefit from inflation above their target (2%) because that inflation reduces the value of the debt incurred by the governments themselves. Looking ahead to 2026, I recommend holding a core position in this ETF, without exceeding the limits of the Kelly criterion and the diversification or objective of each reader.

ETF Overview & Strategy

With regard to the fund's overview, we can see in the first image in this section that the ETF has an AUM of $100 million (small) and an expense ratio of 0.99% per annum, which I consider low given the active management implemented by the fund managers. Its 30-day SEC yield is low (1.31%), as its main objective is long-term capital appreciation and it invests in assets that do not pay dividends or interests. Its investment strategy uses leverage (financial futures) to be constantly 100% invested in the two assets, seeking a notional position of 200%.

The performance table and the premium/discount chart show us the differences between the market price and the fund's NAV throughout its history. As can be seen, it has only traded at a premium or discount above 100 basis points on two occasions, which provides investors with a certain degree of peace of mind and stability when buying or selling their positions.

The Portfolio

The main holdings reflect the dynamics of the ETF's strategy, where gross exposure exceeds 100% (leverage exists). As we can see, exposure to gold is concentrated in the GCG6 Commodity item (gold futures - 93.02%), while exposure to bitcoin is articulated through BTCF6 Currency (BTC futures - 72.68%). In addition to this, the ETF complements these operations with exposure to various ETFs such as (BITO, FBTC, and IBIT). The only part that contributes to the portfolio yield is the money market fund (FGDXX - 29.13%). It can serve as collateral for futures transactions and, with the yield contributed, serve to offset the financing costs of the leverage employed.

Inflation Hedge

As we mentioned in the first section, an increase in the money supply is a problem if the production of goods and services does not grow at the same rate, as it causes imbalances in the prices of those goods, harming the end consumer. The two images above illustrate this point. The first shows how the purchasing power of gold over time tends to behave like a mirror asset compared to the purchasing power of fiat currency. If investors want to maintain their wealth in real terms, they need an asset capable of consistently beating inflation, and gold, as we can see, works well for this purpose.

The second metric represents the evolution of the aggregate M2 indicator by the world's major central banks. As can be seen in the image, the metric has a clear upward trend, which does not decline at any point in time. This is highly related to the global debt dynamics we see today. If the world continues on this path (contained real rates and fiscal necessity) as we are seeing, vehicles such as BTGD, which package two scarce assets with monetary narrative into a single ticker, will be key. For all these reasons, I recommend buying.

Final Thoughts

Comparing the performance of Bitcoin, gold, and BTGD since the end of 2024, we see that the ETF has been the winner of the race, with a cumulative return of 94.45%, compared to 82% for gold and 54% for Bitcoin. This means that, in the short time it has been on the market, it has been able to successfully execute its strategy, undoubtedly helped by the upward trends of the two assets in the period analyzed. I believe that, with proper implementation, vehicles such as this have a long-term future, for the reasons mentioned above. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Fastenal: Dividend Aristocrat With A Moat-Worthy Business
data.description:
There is something to be said about reliable wealth compounders that have delivered strong total returns for shareholders. While tech names like Google (GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), and Apple (AAPL) may come to mind, there are also plenty of non-tech names that fit the bill.

Such I find the case with Fastenal (FAST), which, as shown below, has produced a 444% total return over the past 10 years, far surpassing the 264% of the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. Who says you have to buy Magnificent 7 stocks to see outsized returns?

In this article, I highlight Fastenal, including recent business results, and discuss the merits of owning this stock at the right price, so let’s dig in!

Why FAST?

Fastenal Company is a leading distributor of industrial and construction supplies and is the largest fastener distributor in North America. It has a broad portfolio that spans OEM (original equipment manufacturer) and MRO (maintenance and repair operations) products that are used in manufacturing, infrastructure, and maintenance.

Since its founding almost 60 years ago, Fastenal has evolved far beyond fasteners into a full-service supply chain partner. This includes offering inventory management, vending solutions, e-business platforms, and logistics services across thousands of locations globally. Its local responsiveness combined with global scale enables deep integration with its customers.

FAST strikes me as the type of business Warren Buffett would like, considering its economics of scale. This translates into a moat with above-average margins. FAST scores an ‘A’ grade for Profitability with well above average Gross Profit and EBITDA margins of 45% and 22%, respectively. It also has a high return on total capital of 24%, as shown below.

FAST continued to demonstrate strong results in its Q4 2025 earnings, with net sales growing by 11% YoY to $2.0 billion. For the full year, net sales grew by 9% YoY to hit a record $8.2 billion. This was driven by strong daily sales growth and continued success in expanding relationships with large customers. Net income also rose by an impressive 12% YoY to $294 million during Q4.

This marks FAST’s second consecutive quarter of double-digit growth, which demonstrates its momentum amidst a sluggish industrial economy. Notably, FAST continues to expand into strategic high-value customer sites. This is reflected by the number of active sites generating at least $50K annually growing by 14% YoY. These sites now account for over half of FAST’s revenues. At the same time, FAST is shifting away from low-volume customers who generate under $500 per month in sales.

Also encouraging, FAST’s digital and on-site technology investments are bearing fruit, as nearly half (46.1%) of sales were dispensed through Fastenal Managed Inventory devices. This marks a solid 220 basis points increase from 43.9% in the prior year period. It’s an indication of how deeply embedded FAST’s products are in customers's daily operations, thereby resulting in sticky customer relationships. As shown below, FMI device installations have steadily grown over the past 3 years.

In addition, the e-commerce channel contributed to 30% of sales. Together with FMI, the digital-enabled channel now represents 62% of total sales. This number has steadily grown over the past decade and is meaningful due to the highly efficient nature of these transactions. This contributes to FMI’s expanding operating leverage, with SG&A expense as a percentage of sales declining by 50 bps YoY to 25.4%.

Looking ahead, U.S. manufacturing may see a slowdown in the near term. This is reflected by a slight 0.4 MoM contraction in the manufacturing PMI to 51.8 in December, as shown below. For reference, a PMI over 50 indicates expansion, so it is still in positive territory.

Nonetheless, FMI’s internal sales momentum, particularly among large accounts, is showing promise. FAST’s current President and Chief Sales Officer will assume the CEO role in the middle of this year. On the recent earnings call, the current CEO noted opportunities for expansion across its business units:

I think one thing Fastenal does really well is to our internal teams we point out the possible. There are 240 districts within Fastenal that average around $34 million a year. We look at the business from the standpoint of those 240 business units and we see excellence and strength in that group, and we share the story with all that we see.

And what always is the growth differentiator from district to district is who has the best key account strategy. This is how we think about the future. And as Jeff leads this next chapter of Fastenal, his challenge to the group is, what do we look like when we're a $15 billion organization and what do we need to do to achieve double-digit market share gains in the marketplace.

FAST maintains a strong balance sheet with $1.5 billion in cash and accounts receivable. It also has a very low net debt to TTM EBITDA ratio of 0.1x. This supports the 2% dividend yield, which is covered by a 79% payout ratio. The dividend also has a 5-year CAGR of 12% and 27 consecutive years of growth through all economic cycles, making FAST a Dividend Aristocrat.

My only qualm around FAST is its valuation. At the current price of $42.62, FAST remains richly priced with a forward P/E of 39.8. This is despite it sitting in the middle of its 52-week price range. As shown below, FAST currently sits well above its historical P/E of 30.2.

FAST also trades at a higher valuation than peers. Using EV/EBITDA as an apples-to-apples comparison, since EV includes debt, FAST’s 27x ratio sits well above the 18x and 13x of peers Illinois Tool Works (ITW) and Snap-on Inc. (SNA), as shown below.

Analysts estimate 10% to 12% annual EPS growth over the next two years, which I believe fits in line with a valuation closer to FAST’s historical norm. Potential headwinds like an industrial slowdown due to renewed tariff concerns with Europe could bring down FAST’s valuation to a more favorable value level. As such, I believe investors are better off sitting on the sidelines for now on this great company for a better valuation.

Investor Takeaway

Fastenal is a high-quality industrial stock with a long track record of outperforming the market. It benefits from scale, strong margins, and high returns on capital. FAST is supported by deeply embedded customer relationships driven by managed inventory and digital solutions. FAST also has a very strong balance sheet that supports its growing dividend.

Having said that, much of this strength appears to be already reflected in the stock price. Trading well above its historical valuation and at a premium to peers, FAST offers a limited margin of safety from a stock price standpoint. As such, I rate the stock as a ‘Hold’ at present, as a valuation reset tied to a potential softer industrial backdrop could provide a more attractive entry point.

data.title: Newmont: Turning Looming Geopolitical Crisis Into Shareholder Value
data.description:
Gold prices (XAUUSD:CUR) continue growing, which is a massive tailwind for Newmont Corporation (NEM) as one of the world's largest gold mining companies. The stock experiences strong momentum, and it has gained 35% since my previous bullish writeup. The environment looks favorable for Newmont because gold prices are likely to continue their impressive bull run as investors seek a safe harbor amid mounting geopolitical uncertainty.

Moreover, it is essential to keep in mind that NEM is also a notable copper producer, and this metal's price (HG1:COM) is also growing due to structural tailwinds. With Newmont's proven record of stellar profitability, the company is highly likely to convert favorable commodity prices into real value for shareholders. The forward P/E ratio setup looks extremely bullish, meaning that NEM is likely undervalued. Reiterating a Strong Buy rating for NEM appears to be the most reasonable approach, as I am adding more shares to my existing position.

Fundamental Analysis

If we look at the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (GLD), which tracks the performance of gold prices, we can see that it increased by ~15% since my previous writeup. In addition, we also have to take into account copper prices because NEM also produces this metal in quite large volumes as well. To understand copper price dynamics, we can look at the United States Copper Index Fund ETF (CPER). It increased by around 13% since October 11, which is approximately in line with the gold price dynamic. Meanwhile, Newmont's share price increased by 35% over the same period.

It is highly likely that NEM's share price growth outperformance compared to prices of gold and copper is explained by the company's exceptional profitability. A favorable pricing environment is good for any mining company because it creates tailwinds for revenue growth. However, for value investors, profitability growth is usually significantly more important than revenue growth. And NEM looks great from the profitability perspective. The company doesn't only demonstrate much higher profitability across the board compared to the sector but is also beating its own previous achievements.

If we look at the dynamic over the last five quarters, both gross and operating margins have improved by double-digit percentage points. Newmont's TTM operating margin of 43.5% resembles more of an AI-exposed semiconductor company rather than a metals mining company.

Since the company is so strong in capitalizing on the favorable gold and copper prices, I want my analysis to focus on this crucial factor. I think that the situation is simple and plain for copper prices, as this metal enjoys one apparent structural tailwind. This bullish factor is the global shortage of copper due to the transport electrification megatrend. According to fresh reports, global EV sales jumped by 20% in 2025 compared to 2024. This growth is expected to cool in 2026, but it will remain in the double-digit zone with a forecasted 15.7% growth.

Meanwhile, the global copper production grew by approximately 2.1% in 2025, and it is not an industry where an unexpected production level spike can occur as expansion of production capacity requires years and vast investments in exploration and development of new mines. Therefore, it is no wonder that analysts from S&P Global forecast that the global copper supply gap will notably widen over the next 15 years. It is a strong and long-lasting tailwind for NEM.

However, let's not forget that NEM's core business is gold production. Since the global gold supply is also unlikely to grow rapidly as it requires vast resources to be invested in exploration and building production infrastructure, let's focus on the demand side. The major quality of gold as an investment is its defensive nature as investors consider this precious metal as a safe harbor to weather turbulent times. This confidence stems from the fact that gold is a crucial defensive asset in almost any central bank's portfolio. Despite gold prices setting new records, surveys suggest that 95% of central banks are willing to increase their gold reserves in 2026.

This growing defensive stance of central banks looks fair considering the mounting geopolitical uncertainty. This year started with as many big geopolitical headlines within just first three weeks of January as we sometimes might see over an entire year. The U.S. military operation in Venezuela, a potential direct U.S. vs Iran military conflict, and rapidly worsening relationships within the NATO alliance due to the Greenland controversy. There might be a new chapter of a big trade war between the U.S. and Western Europe in case disputes around Greenland escalate further. And if there are fewer allies within the "collective West," it might lead to some unpredictable geopolitical moves from powerful countries from the East, like Russia and China.

To summarize: our world is on the verge of a big geopolitical crisis (or maybe already experiencing it) and it might result in a global recession. In such a warning environment, it is no wonder that central banks are playing increasingly defensively.

Valuation Analysis

Newmont's forward P/E ratio setup looks extremely bullish. The company's estimated FY2025 forward P/E is expected to shrink notably over the next few years. This trajectory looks reasonable and conservative, given the fact that Wall Street analysts forecast a notable EPS growth deceleration for every next year.

If we look at NEM's Price/Earnings ratio against the sector median and the company's historical averages, the setup looks attractive as well. NEM's P/E is lower than the sector median, despite the stock likely deserves some premium due to its large-scale and exceptional profitability. Moreover, NEM's current P/E ratios are not higher than the last five years' average. I think that currently NEM deserves a notable premium compared to the last five years' average because the company's profitability has been stagnating between 2020 and 2023, which should weigh on the historical average.

Finally, my assertion regarding NEM's compelling valuation is also confirmed by its extremely low forward adjusted PEG ratio of just 0.31, while the sector's is 1.3.

Mitigating Factors

On average, Wall Street analysts disagree with me that NEM is substantially undervalued. Their average target price for NEM is $116.8, which is slightly higher than the last close. It is difficult to say whether the consensus target price from Wall Street analysts will age well or not, but it certainly affects the market's sentiment around the stock. With no near-term upside suggested by the consensus target price, investors might prefer to stay away from NEM or even sell it. Therefore, this factor might be a notable headwind for NEM's share price.

While there are multiple indications that the current geopolitical landscape is favorable for gold prices, it is also fair to say that the environment might change to a less favorable one for defensive assets. For example, we might see sudden positive developments around the Russia-Ukraine war, which will significantly decrease the level of global geopolitical uncertainty. We also see that the U.S. economy is growing faster than expected so far. If the U.S.'s macroeconomic data continues to surpass expectations of experts for longer, it might make investors much less cautious and demand for gold as a defensive asset is likely to decline under this scenario.

Conclusion

As a large producer with stellar profitability, NEM is well positioned to continue capitalizing on growing gold and copper prices and create more value for shareholders. Therefore, NEM remains a Strong Buy.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Trump Takes U-Turn On Greenland Tariffs After Reaching Arctic Framework
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump reverses course after Davos talks, sending stocks sharply higher. (0:14) Sandisk now up 1,000% over six months. (3:02) Natural gas rockets on deep freeze as global energy prices jump. (3:13)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

President Donald Trump has backed off his threat to impose new tariffs in February, after negotiations in Davos over Greenland.

And whether you call it another TACO — Trump Always Chickens Out — or another example of the Art of the Deal, the markets are happy.
Stocks (SPY) are surging, Treasury yields (US10Y) are falling, and the greenback (DXY) is firming.

Trump posted that after a “very productive meeting” with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte, they had “formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region.”

“Based upon this understanding, I will not be imposing the tariffs that were scheduled to go into effect on February 1st,” he added, noting that additional talks are being held concerning the Golden Dome missile-defense concept as it relates to Greenland.

Earlier in his keynote, Trump said he didn’t want to use force, adding:
“All I'm asking for is a piece of ice that can play a role in world protection.”

Also heard in Davos, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said AI has kicked off the largest infrastructure buildout in human history, and warned that the world will need more energy, land, and skilled workers to keep up.

He also praised Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude as a coding tool, saying anyone who runs a software company should use it, and highlighted OpenAI’s (OPENAI) ChatGPT for its success in the consumer space, calling it easy to use and approachable.

Also on the AI front, Meta (META) CTO Andrew Bosworth said the first high-profile AI models built by its Superintelligence Labs team were delivered this month and are already showing “a lot of promise.”

JPMorgan Chase (JPM) CEO Jamie Dimon warned that a proposed Trump plan to cap credit-card interest rates at 10% “would remove credit from 80% of Americans,” adding that the people “crying the most will not be the credit card companies; it will be the restaurants, retailers, travel companies, the schools, the municipalities.”

And Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Deutsche Bank’s CEO personally called to distance the lender from a controversial note suggesting Europeans might sell U.S. assets, saying the bank doesn’t stand by it. That’s not something you see often.

Deutsche Bank said analyst reports don’t necessarily represent the views of senior management, but did not confirm the disavowal.

Among active stocks, Netflix (NFLX) is down after in-line guidance that failed to impress, though analysts are defending the stock.

Wedbush blamed the “underwhelming” report for the weakness, saying investors have grown used to phenomenal results.

Jefferies echoed the bullish stance, expecting Netflix to remain the dominant streaming player and to grow free cash flow at about 15% a year over the next five years.

And Morgan Stanley noted Netflix surpassed 325M members in 2025, adding more than 25M net new subscribers—more than any other major streaming service, despite already having the largest base.

Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) reported better-than-expected Q4 revenue, driven by its Pharma and MedTech divisions, but adjusted earnings for the quarter missed expectations.

J&J also rolled out its 2026 outlook, guiding to EPS of $11.53 on $100.5B in reported sales—both above forecasts.

And Sandisk (SNDK) is up again, extending a ridiculous six-month rally that has seen shares climb more than 1,000% as it pivots from general storage to a high-performance, AI-focused brand.

In today’s trading, U.S. natural gas futures continue their massive gains, now up more than 50% in two days as freezing temperatures across much of the country drive heating demand.

Front-month Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) for February delivery is up about 20%, hitting a fresh YTD high above $4.65/MMBtu, and contracts are on track for their biggest weekly gain in more than 35 years.

Cold weather has sent energy prices sharply higher worldwide. Japan’s power prices have hit a three-month high, and European gas futures are up nearly 30% so far this month. Snow is also forecast this weekend in Texas, home to key gas production sites—raising the risk of temporary outages and lower exports.

data.title: Micron: Are We At The Peak Of The Supercycle? Not Even Close
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to take a look at Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), which has performed tremendously since my last article back in September, returning over 130%, and even better since my article in August, topping 230% performance vs. SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust’s (SPY) 6%. I believe that with such multiple contractions, there is still a lot of room left to keep going, but I am going to give it a little downgrade to a Buy, as I am quite heavily weighted towards this AI growth story and will pick up some more shares only if we see some sort of a pullback due to macroeconomic jitters or people taking profits.

Briefly on the latest performance: massive multiples contraction

Micron’s operations have continued to improve tremendously since the last time I covered them back in September, and in such a short amount of time, with the biggest change coming from the increase in average selling price of the company’s products due to the shortages in bottlenecks created by the power-hungry AI data centers and anything to do with AI.

Sales saw a boost of around 56% for Q1 ’26, which is already an impressive growth; however, what is more remarkable is the company’s gross margin, which went from around 38.5% last year to over 56% this quarter. It is very impressive to see that the company managed to keep its expenses low while sales skyrocketed, leading to massive operational improvement across the board. It does look like most of the top-line growth came from an increase in ASP rather than manufacturing output because there is no way that the company can keep its operating expenses and COGS.

The earnings slides show us that the company’s bit shipments were “up slightly Q/Q” for DRAM, and for NAND, bit shipments increased by mid-to-high single-digit % Q/Q. These align with the company’s increase in COGS and OpEx, which means the rest of the tailwinds came from a massive increase in ASPs. ASPs increased from mid-teens to 20% sequentially alone.

So, if we take a look at what prices were back in December of 2024 for these products, we will see that ASP surged around 400% by Q4 ’25, according to this article. Of course, many fabs were also underutilized before this growth story, which meant that the fixed costs were also spread out a lot more efficiently recently, leading to a massive improvement in profitability. Even though MU doesn’t disclose its utilization rates, we can safely assume the company is running at full capacity, which is still not enough since ASPs continue to surge. The company wouldn’t be increasing its capex and building more fabs if it still had the capacity to produce.

The company is seeing growth across all its business units, but the one that particularly stands out is, of course, the Cloud Memory business unit, which doubled in a year on the back of the massive demand for memory solutions by hyperscalers and high bandwidth memory, or HBM, for data centers. Core Data is also showing some decent acceleration recently, since this unit has a lot of exposure to data center needs for SSDs.

So, these results pushed the company’s margins and bottom line to levels it hasn’t seen in a long time. In terms of profitability in pure earnings value, this is a record for the company, while the 2018 and 2021 super cycles pushed the company’s forward P/E down as low as 4x-6x.

Even right now, the company’s FW P/E ratio sits at just over 11x, which is ridiculously cheap for such a growth story. For 2026, the company is expecting to earn around $33 a share, and analysts are still expecting further growth in 2027 to around $41.50, which would bring its FW P/E ratio down to under 9x based on the current share price.

Supercycle to last longer: here’s why

Even after such an amazing run that Micron had over the last year, I don’t think this is the top, and it may not be the top for at least another couple of years.

The unprecedented demand for energy by hyperscalers and other big tech is pushing every company that is there to help them achieve their AI superiority to have no choice but to comply. Of course, companies like MU, Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), and other manufacturers are going to oblige. There is no way they are going to leave any money on the table if it is literally just being handed to them hand over fist, and they can’t keep up. Micron is upping its capex spending because it just has to in order to meet the big tech demand. All hyperscalers came out last quarter and said that they are not releasing their foot off the pedal just yet when it comes to AI capex spending. Hundreds of billions were spent on AI in 2025, and this is only going to increase this year, too.

Naturally, MU has upped its FY26 capex guidance to $20B because the HBM/DRAM capacity has been sold out already for 2026. And honestly, I don’t think this is going to be the end of CapEx increases. I wouldn’t be surprised if MU comes out with additional increases in the near future. So, what is the company using this capital for?

In the most recent conference call, the management said that they are working on two Fabs in Idaho. Fab 1 was pulled forward and expects to see wafer output in mid-27. Fab 2 will begin construction in 2026, with the intention to be fully operational by the end of 2028.

MU also plans to do a major expansion project in Japan, worth $9.6B specifically for HBM, which is said to start construction in May ’26 and finish in 2028.

The latest development is that the company has also begun work on its $100B NY megafab, aiming to produce 40% of its DRAM domestically by 2040. This project is supposed to be completed by Q2 ’28, with another Fab beginning construction in H2 ’28 for completion by late 2033.

Competitors in this space are also running at full capacity and are all sold out through 2026, which means they are all looking to build out more capacity in the future to meet the demand. SK hynix (HXSC.F) is accelerating its investments in additional fabs to meet the demand, with a $12.9B investment in Korea, aiming to be operational by the end of 2027. Samsung (SSNLF) is adding a chip production line in Korea as well to meet the global demand for AI, which should come online sometime in 2028.

There’s a theme here. Capacity takes quite a long time to become available. What we can see is that most of this capacity is not going to come in for at least another 2 years if SK Hynix’s plans go accordingly. Overall, there seems to be a solid 3 years of undersupply, which means these players are going to benefit greatly for a little while longer. We can see from the analysts’ EPS projections above that they coincide with this timeline. Online publications like this one see the bottlenecks lasting until 2028 as well. In short, the super cycle is here to stay.

Valuation

Such an improvement warrants an update on its valuation model. Below are the assumptions I used.

We can see a massive increase in sales for the next year is in store, and a gradual slowdown in the years after is projected by the analysts, so I went with that too. On operating margins, in order for me to get to around $33 in EPS for 2026, I had to use 58%, which is going to increase a bit the next year and gradually start to come down as capacity unlocks and bottlenecks disappear. Capex will continue to be elevated for the first few years and then gradually come down.

With these assumptions, we get the following EPS projections.

I am also using the company’s WACC of around 10%, which I think is sufficient. With that said, Micron’s fair value is around $430 a share, which means there is still some room for growth if we look at the fundamentals alone.

Risks

The company is going to experience a massive downcycle; it is inevitable. Hyperscalers will eventually stop increasing their capex budgets, and when they do. These memory players will see a massive decrease in earnings and demand for their products, especially when there is going to be a lot more capacity available in two to three years.

My model doesn’t account for the drop in profitability because it is hard to tell what is going to happen after 2028. There may be more demand, which will help its earnings, or the earnings will halve. So, take it with a grain of salt.

Competition from the two biggest players in the space, SK Hynix and Samsung, may take a lot of the share from MU, which will lead to underutilization in the company’s fabs, which then leads to a massive operational imbalance and lower profitability.

Closing Comments

I will continue to ride the AI wave and firmly believe that $430 is not the top for MU, given how cheaply it is trading right now. My little position grew so quickly that I couldn’t grow it into a much bigger one over the last month or so, as it seemed that there were no red days to take advantage of.

I will be looking to add to my position, most likely before the company reports in March, as I think we will see a lot more upside going forward. There is room for this stock to continue its run thanks to the super cycle that is to last at least another 2 years, so I will enjoy the ride.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: QQQX Vs. QQQI: The CEF Is Relatively More Attractive For Those OK With Some Complexity
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Nuveen Nasdaq 100 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (QQQX) provides investors with exposure to the Nasdaq 100 Index while also employing a call overwrite strategy. The call overlay strategy helps to deliver a relatively higher distribution yield to investors through collecting option premiums. As a closed-end fund, QQQX can trade at a market price wildly different from its underlying net asset value per share, which can create potential opportunities. We last covered QQQX in August 2025, and the fund has put up solid performance since then, even as its discount has widened a touch.

With that said, the Neso Nasdaq 100 High Income (QQQI) ETF is a relatively newer entrant that provides quite a similar strategy. We've covered that fund before, and it is one that remains on my watchlist. I may begin to dollar-cost average in, but I was hoping for a correction or at least a pullback before wanting to get too aggressive.

With QQQX, it continues to trade at an attractive discount to its NAV, making it a choice worth considering. One of the cautions for both would be that the funds being exposed to the Nasdaq 100 could suggest some underlying portfolio overvaluation in a stretched market. Primarily, the heavy exposure to the Magnificent 7 names that continue to drive the broader indexes higher can also lead to some concentration concerns.

QQQX Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.34
  • Discount/Premium: -8.22%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.83%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.89%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $1.523 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual CEF

QQQX's investment objective is to "offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total returns with less volatility than the Nasdaq 100 Index..."

In an effort to achieve this, the fund will invest:

...in a U.S. equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the Nasdaq 100 Index, as well as selling call options on the 35%-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns. The strategy will consider the Fund's tax position and employ techniques to improve after-tax shareholder outcomes.

QQQI Basics

  • Dividend Frequency: Monthly
  • Dividend Yield: 14.11% (0.02% SEC yield)
  • Expense Ratio: 0.68%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $7.561 billion
  • Structure: Active ETF

QQQI's investment objective is to "generate high monthly income in a tax-efficient manner with the potential for equity appreciation." They will attempt to do that by:

investing in a portfolio of stocks that make up the Nasdaq-100® Index (the "Nasdaq-100®" or the "Reference Index") and a call options strategy, that consists of written (sold) call options on the Nasdaq-100® Index ("NDX® call options"). The Fund seeks to generate high monthly income from the premiums earned from the NDX® call options as well as the dividends received from the Fund's equity holdings.

QQQX Performance Since Prior Update

Since our prior update, the fund has performed quite well. It has outperformed the S&P 500 Index, even while we have seen its discount widen a touch since our prior update. With the discount widening, that would otherwise drive the total share price returns lower relative to the total NAV returns—which is what reflects the underlying portfolio performance.

With that said, one of the reasons for this is because of the mega-cap tech names, looking at the Mag 7 names again, performing quite strongly. We can see that reflected in the performance between comparing QQQX, Invesco QQQ (QQQ), and Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS). QQQX and QQQ performed quite similarly on both the total share price and NAV return basis, but MAGS outperformed.

With a call writing fund such as QQQX, it can face an upside cap when the market is rallying sharply higher. However, QQQX managed to beat the index during this measurement period, albeit relatively modestly. A beat nonetheless, though. Part of the reason for this is that QQQX utilizes a "dynamic" approach, as its name would suggest.

They can employ their option writing overlay strategy to varying degrees depending on the manager's outlook, and that can include how far out-of-the-money the options are written as well. They also incorporate writing options on indexes and ETFs, which can include not only the Nasdaq 100 and QQQ but also the S&P 500 Index.

QQQI Comparison

This all makes it quite similar to QQQI.

QQQX invests in the underlying holdings, with its latest top ten comprising 57.6% of the fund. Interestingly, though, Apple (AAPL) is the largest position in the fund over NVIDIA (NVDA).

Similarly, but with NVDA as the largest holding, QQQI also invests in individual holdings. The top ten here comes to 50.91% allocation of the fund's capital, a bit less concentrated relative to QQQX, but still quite heavily concentrated regardless.

QQQX actually holds a greater number of holdings, at nearly 200, compared to QQQI's ~100. This likely reflects the fact that QQQX employs writing call options on some S&P 500 Index exposure as well. In the latest full holdings list, they even include some short Russell 2000 index call exposure as well.

Invesco QQQ Trust Series 1 QQQ US 12/19/25 C630 -0.02% (120,000) $2.39 ($286,642.44) Equity Option
NASDAQ 100 Stock INDEX NDX US 12/19/25 C24000 -0.52% (14,000) $567.72 ($7,948,121.67) Index Option

For these funds, the ability for them to partially overwrite dynamically and at various strike prices reduces the upside cap that we see from other traditional call-writing funds.

Going back to the inception of QQQI, QQQX has outperformed on a total share price basis due to the discount variable that QQQI does not have to navigate to the same degree. This is because the ETF structure has a creation/redemption mechanism where its outstanding shares are created or destroyed to keep the share price pegged to its NAV. With that said, we can also see that QQQI has outperformed slightly on a total NAV return basis.

This is one of the advantages of CEFs over ETFs, though some investors see this more as an annoyance. QQQX has traded at a significant discount for the last couple of years now when it previously traded at a premium fairly regularly. This could be due to greater competition in the ETF space, and this could easily become the new 'normal,' but this still makes QQQX look relatively more attractive.

A more patient investor can wait for further potential upside relative to something like QQQI, which is delivering similar results, so it wouldn't take all that much of a discount widening for QQQX to continue to outperform on a total share price basis.

For what it is worth, this is one of the reasons why some CEF investors get frustrated, though. Had they bought 2-3 years ago, they would not have seen similarly strong results that the underlying portfolio would have delivered. Albeit, it has still delivered strong total market price returns thanks to an incredible 3-year historical run.

For investors that don't want to deal with that additional layer of complexity, QQQI seems like a solid choice. I, personally, am looking to invest in both at some point.

Distributions

One final point I'll touch on briefly is the distribution rates that these funds pay. They are significantly higher than what one would get from QQQ. However, QQQI pays monthly, and the latest distribution rate comes in at 14.11%. The payout fluctuates each month based on how much the fund has taken in for option premiums and dividends collected. That said, thanks to a very strong overall broader market performance to help keep QQQI's NAV steady or growing, the payout has also been incredibly stable.

Should volatility finally eventually pick up for more than a very brief downturn, as we saw in April 2025, I fully suspect that the payout would start to vary more wildly.

For some evidence of that, QQQX is a much older fund that goes back to even before the Global Financial Crisis. They pay a managed level distribution whereby they look to pay out a fixed amount for a period of time. As we see historically, this can be adjusted as needed, and that can sometimes be as little as often, only a couple of quarters. Further, the fund pays on a quarterly distribution schedule with the latest rate of 7.83% (7.19% based on NAV).

I suspect the fund is due for an increase in its quarterly distribution—but perhaps the management team and board here are being more cautious given the market is regularly hitting all-time highs. With a lower distribution rate being paid, it means less potential for a cut in the future.

Conclusion

QQQX has provided a solid track record as a call writing fund with a heavy leaning tilt toward the Nasdaq 100. The fund also employs a dynamic overwrite strategy that provides writing calls against various indexes and ETFs, which also gives its underlying portfolio some varying exposure. One of the more appealing aspects of opportunity is the fund's hefty discount to NAV at the moment.

Of course, this comes with a massive caveat: this may never close in the future, or it could even widen. That would negate the opportunity for what could be seen as more disappointing results, which is what investors would have seen if investing when it was at a double-digit premium. I didn't personally pick it up until last year when it started trading at a wide discount, but I can still empathize.

For investors who do not want to deal with the added layer of discount/premium watching, QQQI is also a solid choice for income-focused investors. Due to the ETF structure, the fund should rarely trade at any meaningful discount/premium for any extended period of time. It delivers distributions monthly and at a higher current rate. The fund further takes a more plain approach with its portfolio, reducing flexibility, but you know pretty much exactly what you are getting at any time with the fund. They hold the Nasdaq 100 names and write laddered index calls around a month out but can be overwritten to varying degrees given management's outlook.

That varies a bit differently from QQQX, which can hold a greater number of holdings and overwrite indexes and ETFs outside the Nasdaq 100 alone. However, it is similar in that it can also adjust its option overlay degree based on how overwritten the portfolio is and select various strike prices.
